Name: MIT Rocket Team
Relevance: Student team building and launching high-power rockets, including Spaceport America Cup vehicles and liquid-engine / flight-electronics work.

Name: Students for the Exploration and Development of Space (MIT SEDS)
Relevance: MIT’s SEDS chapter runs social/outreach activity and technical space projects; it is developing the ADORE 1U CubeSat with electrospray thrusters from MIT’s Space Propulsion Lab.

Name: MIT Satellite Team
Relevance: Undergraduate-led satellite hub focused on satellite systems, software, and hardware engineering, with backing from AeroAstro and the Small Satellite Collaborative.

Name: MIT Astrogazers
Relevance: Graduate-student-led astronomy outreach group that brings telescopes into the community for public observing and astronomy engagement.

Name: Small Satellite Collaborative
Relevance: MIT organization supporting research and education on small satellites, including missions, subsystems, payloads, and enabling technologies.

Name: Space Systems Laboratory (SSL)
Relevance: Space-systems lab focused on design and integration of future space systems; its expertise includes ADCS, avionics, communications, payloads, and small satellites.

Name: Space Propulsion Laboratory (SPL)
Relevance: MIT lab conducting propulsion-system engineering, electric propulsion characterization, electrospray thruster fabrication, and broader spacecraft systems engineering.

Name: Space Telecommunications, Astronomy and Radiation Laboratory (STAR Lab)
Relevance: STAR Lab develops small-spacecraft capabilities spanning optical communications, astrophysical instrumentation, remote sensing, and in-space assembly.

Name: ARCLab (Astrodynamics, Space Robotics, and Controls Lab)
Relevance: ARCLab explicitly works at the intersection of astrodynamics, autonomy, and controls to support space exploration.

Name: MIT Kavli Institute for Astrophysics and Space Research
Relevance: Major MIT institute for astrophysics and space research, combining space and ground-based instrumentation with astrophysics research.

Name: MIT Haystack Observatory
Relevance: MIT interdisciplinary observatory doing radio astronomy, geodesy, and atmospheric science; clearly relevant for astronomy and some remote-sensing-adjacent work.

Name: Wallace Astrophysical Observatory
Relevance: MIT optical observatory used for undergraduate teaching and research in astronomy/astrophysics.

Name: Human Systems Laboratory (HSL)
Relevance: AeroAstro lab studying human physiological and cognitive performance for spacecraft and other systems; its work includes spacesuits, planetary mission resource utilization, space teleoperation, and artificial gravity.

Name: Space Enabled
Relevance: Danielle Wood’s group uses designs enabled by space technology, with background in satellite design, Earth science applications, systems engineering, and space policy.

Name: MIT Space Exploration Initiative
Relevance: Media Lab–affiliated initiative creating and deploying space technologies and explicitly focused on democratizing access to space exploration.

Name: Aerospace Controls Laboratory (ACL)
Relevance: ACL works on autonomous systems and control design for aircraft, spacecraft, and ground vehicles, making it strongly relevant for guidance, navigation, and control.

Name: SPARK Lab
Relevance: SPARK develops robotics and autonomy methods for air, space, and ground applications; relevant for space robotics, perception, and navigation.

Name: Aerospace Plasma Group
Relevance: AeroAstro plasma group includes coursework and research tied to low-temperature plasmas, including plasma propulsion.

Name: MIT Department of Earth, Atmospheric and Planetary Sciences (EAPS)
Relevance: EAPS research explicitly includes exoplanets, planetary formation, solar system bodies, space instrumentation, and remote sensing.

Name: EAPS Program in Planetary Science (PPS)
Relevance: MIT’s planetary science program covers solar-system and exoplanet studies using telescopic observation, spacecraft data, orbital mechanics, astrodynamics, and remote sensing.

Name: Planetary Science + Astronomy concentration (EAPS)
Relevance: Undergraduate concentration centered on planetary science and astronomy; relevant as a student-facing MIT unit for space-science training.

Name: Astronomy @ MIT
Relevance: MIT’s umbrella astronomy/astrophysics program spans Physics, EAPS, and AeroAstro, connecting multiple university-affiliated astronomy and planetary efforts.

Name: Physics Astrophysics Observation, Instrumentation, and Experiment
Relevance: Physics research area focused on astrophysics across many wavebands and gravitational waves, with instrument development for ground and space studies.

Name: Varon Lab
Relevance: Uses satellite remote sensing and computing to study atmospheric composition; not space-systems work, but clearly adjacent through satellite data exploitation.

Name: MIT Planetary Astronomy Laboratory (PAL)
Relevance: EAPS/astronomy group studying the solar system with telescopes and multiple observational techniques.

Name: Robust Robotics Group
Relevance: Builds unmanned aerial vehicles and other autonomous robots that can operate without GPS or maps; highly relevant for autonomous flight and space-adjacent robotics methods.

Name: Reliable Autonomous Systems Lab @ MIT (REALM)
Relevance: Listed by AeroAstro among its labs, and likely relevant to autonomous aerospace systems, but the retrieved material did not directly confirm specific space/UAV focus in the source snippets reviewed.

Name: Autonomy and Embedded Robotics Accelerated (AERA) Research Group
Relevance: Appears in AeroAstro’s lab listings and likely overlaps with autonomous aerial/embedded systems, but I did not verify a clearly space-specific or UAV-specific mission statement from the sources reviewed.

Name: Minor in Astronomy
Relevance: MIT officially offers the minor jointly through EAPS and Physics; it is clearly astronomy-related, but it is an academic program rather than a standalone group/lab/team.
